seoTitle: How to Integrate Trello With GitHub
metaDescription: Connect GitHub repositories to Trello using the native GitHub Power-Up to track branches, commits, and pull requests on your cards.
intro: Keeping non-technical teammates and developers on the same page means giving everyone visibility into code progress without forcing a browser-tab shuffle. This guide covers connecting GitHub to Trello so branches, commits, and pull requests show up directly on your cards.

steps.heading: Open the Power-Up directory
steps.body: On your board, click Power-Ups to browse available integrations.

steps.heading: Add the GitHub Power-Up
steps.body: Search for GitHub, select the official listing, and click Add.

steps.heading: Authorize cross-platform access
steps.body: Approve the permissions needed to bridge your GitHub organization profile with your Atlassian workspace.

steps.heading: Link a repository
steps.body: Connect a specific public or private repository to your board through the Power-Up configuration.

steps.heading: Attach development items to cards
steps.body: Open a card and attach relevant branches, commits, issues, or pull requests directly to it.

steps.heading: Track status badges
steps.body: Check the front of your cards for color-coded status badges reflecting live code checks.

faq.question: Can I connect a private GitHub repository?
faq.answer: Yes, the GitHub Power-Up supports linking both public and private repositories, provided you authorize the correct permissions.

faq.question: What GitHub items can I attach to a card?
faq.answer: You can attach branches, commits, issues, and active pull requests directly to a Trello card.

faq.question: How will I know if a pull request check fails?
faq.answer: The card front displays a color-coded status badge that reflects the current state of the linked GitHub item.

faq.question: Do non-developers need GitHub access to see this?
faq.answer: No, once attached, the status is visible on the Trello card without needing direct GitHub access.
